Description: An air quality sample collected using a dichotomous sampler that collects airborne particles in two distinctive size categories fine (PM2.5) and coarse (PM2.5 – 10).
A sample of settling particles (dust) collected over time using an open container.
Emissions of air from a stack or similar apparatus.
A sample of airborne fluoride compounds on a treated surface or filter. The collected sample is later analyzed chemically to determine fluoride concentration in the air.
An air sample collected using a high-volume (HiVol) air sampler to draw large volumes of air through a filter.
Same as Air-HiVol but a selective size inlet is used to collect only particles below a specific diameter (e.g. PM10).
An air sample collected using a low-volume (LoVol) air sample to airborne particleson a filter.
All mediums ending in ‘Other’ should only be used in extenuating circumstances. Contact the EnMoDSHelp@gov.bc.ca before using ‘Other’ mediums.
An air sample collected using a polyurethane foam filter (PUF) to capture airborne semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) and particulates.
Emissions of air from a stack or similar apparatus.
A sample of airborne sulphur compounds which react on a coated plate to form measurable sulfate.
Not be used, only for historical samples.
Bottom-dwelling invertebrate aquatic organisms. Can be from hard (rock) or soft sediment, including organisms that live within the sediments.
A sample of fish body tissue, can included attributes of individual fish such as species, weight, size, or tissue type.
All mediums ending in ‘Other’ should only be used in extenuating circumstances. Contact the EnMoDSHelp@gov.bc.ca before using ‘Other’ mediums.
Not be used, only for historical samples.
Zooplankton are microscopic or small drifting animals in aquatic environments. They feed on phytoplankton or other zooplankton.
Aquatic macrophytes are large, visible plants in water bodies, including submerged, floating, or emergent species.
All mediums ending in ‘Other’ should only be used in extenuating circumstances. Contact the EnMoDSHelp@gov.bc.ca before using ‘Other’ mediums.
Aquatic periphyton is a community of algae, bacteria, and microorganisms attached to submerged surfaces.
Phytoplankton are microscopic, photosynthetic organisms drifting in water.
A land-based organism that performs photosynthesis.
Not be used, only for historical samples.
Nutrient-rich organic materials produced from treated sewage sludge. They are processed to reduce pathogens and contaminant, then applied to soil as fertilizer or soil conditioner.
Particles of organic matter and minerals that settle at the bottom of water bodies.
The fine, powdery residue from burning coal or biomass in boilers. Fly ash is carried with flue gases, while grate ash collects at the furnace bottom.
All mediums ending in ‘Other’ should only be used in extenuating circumstances. Contact the EnMoDSHelp@gov.bc.ca before using ‘Other’ mediums.
Discarded, non-liquid waste from households, industries, or businesses.

Sludges are semi-solid residues from industrial, municipal, or wastewater treatment processes. They contain water, organic matter, and contaminants. Sludges for beneficial land application, see biosolids.
Terrestrial based sediments. Contains organic matter, minerals, air and water. Different from bottom sediments which come from the bottom of water bodies.
Not be used, only for historical samples.
A mix of fresh and saltwater, typically found in estuaries or coastal areas. It has salinity between freshwater and seawater.
Water with low dissolved salt content, found in rivers, lakes, and streams.
Water stored beneath the Earth's surface in soil pores and rock formations. It supplies wells, springs, and aquifers.
Liquid that drains or leaches through waste materials, dissolving contaminants. Commonly from landfills, it can carry pollutants into soil and groundwater.
Saltwater found in oceans and seas, characterized by high salinity.
All mediums ending in ‘Other’ should only be used in extenuating circumstances. Contact the EnMoDSHelp@gov.bc.ca before using ‘Other’ mediums.
Any form of water such as rain, snow, sleet, or hail, that falls from the atmosphere.
Runoff from rain or melting snow that flows over land surfaces, streets, and roofs, collecting pollutants.
A thin layer of organic or inorganic material floating on the water’s surface.
Not be used, only for historical samples.
Used water from homes, industries, or businesses containing impurities and pollutants. It includes sewage and process water.
